ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for unagi gyudon with beef and half boiled egg. FOODON_00002678 (rice bowl food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Rice dish) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for unagi gyudon with beef and egg combination.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Japanese rice bowl combination.

main_ingredients: tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with grilled unagi, simmered sliced beef, a half boiled egg, and accompanied by miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef rice bowl) and unagi donburi component analysis — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods; supplemented with Japanese food composition data (Standard Tables of Food Composition in Japan 2020, MEXT)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Japan (MEXT)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact combination dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: steamed white rice (~55% by weight), grilled unagi (~15%), simmered beef (~18%), half boiled egg (~9%), tare sauce (~3%). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for rice (FDC ID 168878), beef (FDC ID 174032), and egg (FDC ID 173424). MEXT Japan Standard Tables of Food Composition 2020 used for grilled unagi (eel, freshwater, broiled). Values represent a blended per-100g estimate for the combined bowl excluding miso soup.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented with University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated at 64 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database; white rice, boiled, average GI 64). The protein and fat content from beef, eel, and egg will moderately attenuate the overall glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 112.75) / 100 ≈ 72.2, rounded to 72.8 using 550 g serving × 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 112.75 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from tare sauce (soy-based) and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 310 mg per 100 g, equating to roughly 1,700 mg per full serving), which substantially contributes to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or omitting miso soup can partially mitigate sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from large portion of white rice and added sugars in tare sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish as the estimated glycemic load per serving is approximately 72 (high), driven by a substantial white rice base and sugar-containing tare sauce; portion reduction, substitution with brown rice where available, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables are recommended strategies to moderate postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and egg yolk, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from unagi
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware that this dish contains moderate saturated fat (~1.9 g per 100 g) from beef and egg, but the grilled unagi component provides beneficial long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which may support cardiovascular health; overall sodium content remains a significant concern and portion control is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Boiled Egg Unagi Bowl with Beef (半熟卵うなぎ牛丼, Hanjuku Tamago Unagi Gyudon) is a Japanese donburi (rice bowl) dish combining two classic Japanese preparations: unagi donburi (grilled freshwater eel over rice) and gyudon (simmered beef over rice), topped with a half boiled (soft-boiled) egg. The dish is served in a deep bowl with a generous base of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, layered with kabayaki-style grilled unagi (eel glazed with sweet tare sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar), thinly sliced simmered beef cooked in a similar sweet-savoury tare, and a half boiled egg with a soft, runny yolk. It is typically accompanied by miso soup or a beverage. The flavour profile is rich, sweet, and savoury with umami depth from both the eel and beef; the egg adds creaminess. Texturally, the dish contrasts the tender, flaky eel with the soft beef and the silky egg yolk over sticky rice. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, ~924 kcal per full serving), providing significant protein (~9.2 g/100 g) from multiple animal sources, moderate fat (~5.8 g/100 g), and a high carbohydrate load (~20.5 g/100 g) predominantly from white rice. Unagi is a notable source of vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin B12, and omega-3 fatty acids. Clinically, the dish carries a high glycemic load (~72 per serving) and high sodium content (~1,700 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets. This dish is popular in Japanese fast-casual restaurant chains such as Sukiya and is available in Singapore via food delivery platforms.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a unagi beef bowl?
What is in a half boiled egg unagi bowl?
Is unagi bowl high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and MEXT Japan Standard Tables of Food Composition 2020 for individual components (rice, beef, eel, egg). GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (white rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated for the combined bowl (excluding miso soup) using a weight-based blend: steamed white rice ~55%, grilled unagi ~15%, simmered beef ~18%, half boiled egg ~9%, tare sauce ~3%. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese fast-casual donburi portion sizes; actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. Sodium estimate of 310 mg/100 g reflects tare sauce contribution; actual sodium may be higher if miso soup is included in consumption. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component analysis and are not directly reported from a single source entry; these values carry higher uncertainty. 5. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on white rice as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database; the protein and fat co-ingestion will likely attenuate the actual glycemic response. 6. The dish is not halal-certified as typically prepared (pork-derived mirin may be present in some formulations; eel is considered makruh or impermissible in some Islamic jurisprudence schools). 7. Input food_name contained a menu item number prefix '981.' which has been removed for canonical naming. 8. cuisine_tags in the input included 'Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers', 'Drinks & Beverages', and 'Fried Chicken' which are restaurant-level tags not applicable to this specific dish and have been excluded.
